summary: Cantrill reflects on how corporate disdain for customers drove him from Oracle after the Sun acquisition, using a recent conversation with a former colleague now at Broadcom to illustrate how this toxic attitude persists in tech acquisitions. The colleague's dismissive 'some that tried came crawling back lol' comment about VMware customers epitomizes the contempt Cantrill finds repugnant. He argues that companies built on customer coercion cannot sustain innovation and will not endure, contrasting this with Oxide's explicit mission to love its customers.
key_points: Customer contempt was the primary reason Cantrill left Oracle after Sun acquisition
Broadcom's VMware acquisition mirrors Oracle's customer-hostile approach
Being sued by customers is shameful and indicates a broken relationship
Coercion-based business models are antithetical to innovation
Enduring companies are built on genuine customer love, not lock-in
money_quotes: I could not stomach the disdain that Oracle had for its own customers.
That last sentence—'some that tried came crawling back lol'—made my stomach turn, reminding me viscerally of the contempt that Oracle had for its own customers that I had found so loathsome.
Suing your customers is gross—and being sued by your customers is shameful.
Companies that have disdain for their own customers will be reviled in return. Such companies may be able to thrive in the short term, but they do not endure in the limit.
When coercion is your business model, innovation is not merely unnecessary but actively antithetical.
We aspired to be 'the kind of company that customers will love to buy from—and employees will be proud to work for.'
key_insight: Companies that treat customer lock-in as a business model rather than earning customer trust through innovation are building on a foundation that cannot endure.
